claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit in the UK.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and the child or children you're asking about.

Let me start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about Child Benefit for?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 16 years old and lives with me here at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm assessing Child Benefit eligibility for your son Alex who is 16 years old and lives with you.

Since Alex is 16, I need to ask about his education or training status, as this affects eligibility for children aged 16 and over.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** 

This includes things like:
- Full-time non-advanced education (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or equivalent)
- Approved vocational training courses
- Education at school or college

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he actually just left school recently. He's in that stage where he's just finished his GCSEs and is trying to figure out what to do next. He's signed up with the local careers service to help him work out his options - you know, whether to go back to college, look for work, or what direction to take.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining Alex's situation. It sounds like he's in a transitional period after finishing his GCSEs.

I need to clarify a few more details:

**When exactly did Alex leave school or finish his education?** (For example, did he finish at the end of the summer term, or a specific month?)

This timing is important because there are specific rules about how long Child Benefit can continue after a young person leaves education, especially if they're registered with careers services during that transition period.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He finished at the end of the summer term, so that would have been back in late June when the school year ended. It's been a few months now since he left.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that detail. So Alex finished school at the end of June, and it's now been a few months.

One more important question: **Has Alex enrolled in or started any approved education or training since leaving school in June?** 

For example, has he:
- Started a college course (like A-levels, BTECs, or other courses)?
- Begun an apprenticeship or training program?
- Gone back into any form of education?

Or is he still registered with the careers service but hasn't yet started any education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't started anything yet. He's still just registered with the careers service, working out what he wants to do. He hasn't enrolled in any college courses or apprenticeships or anything like that - just trying to figure out his next steps really.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me ask one more question to complete my assessment:

**Approximately how long has it been since Alex left school in June?** In other words, what is the current month now? 

This is important because there are specific time limits for how long Child Benefit can continue after a young person leaves education, even if they're registered with careers services.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's now the beginning of November, so it's been about 4 months since he finished school in June.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

**For Alex (age 16):**
Alex left school at the end of June after completing his GCSEs. While he is registered with the careers service, he hasn't enrolled in or started any approved education or training since then.

Under UK Child Benefit rules, when a young person aged 16-19 leaves approved education or training, Child Benefit can continue for a limited extension period. This extension runs until **the end of August** following the date they leave education (known as the 'terminal date'). This gives young people time to find work or arrange further education or training.

Since Alex left school in June, your Child Benefit entitlement for him would have ended at the end of August. As it's now November, the extension period has expired, and without Alex being in approved education or training, you are no longer eligible for Child Benefit for him.

**Important:** If Alex does enroll in approved education or training in the future (such as starting a college course or apprenticeship), you may be able to claim Child Benefit again, so it's worth reapplying if his circumstances change.

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and left approved education at the end of June. It is now early November, approximately 4 months later. While Alex is registered with the careers service, he has not enrolled in or started any approved education or training since leaving school. Under UK Child Benefit rules, when a young person aged 16-19 leaves approved education or training, Child Benefit can continue for a limited extension period to allow them to find work or arrange further education/training. This extension period runs until the end of August following the date they leave education (known as the 'terminal date'). Since Alex left school in June, the extension period would have ended at the end of August. As it is now November and Alex is not in approved education or training, the claimant is no longer eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, as the extension period has expired.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 16), who left school in June after completing GCSEs. While Alex is registered with careers services, he has not started any approved education or training. Child Benefit eligibility ended at the end of August (the terminal date), as the extension period for young people leaving education expired. Since it is now November, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

This extension period runs until the end of August following the date they leave education (known as the 'terminal date'). Since Alex left school in June, the extension period would have ended at the end of August. As it is now November and Alex is not in approved education or training, the claimant is no longer eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, as the extension period has expired.

While Alex is registered with careers services, he has not started any approved education or training. Child Benefit eligibility ended at the end of August (the terminal date), as the extension period for young people leaving education expired. Since it is now November, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
